topic.title: How Neural Networks Learn
topic.summary: Neural networks learn by adjusting connection weights through backpropagation and gradient descent.

topics.content_md:
## How Neural Networks Learn

Imagine your brain learning to recognise a cat. Each time you see a cat and someone confirms "yes, that's a cat", your brain strengthens certain connections. Neural networks work the same way.

A neural network is made up of **layers of nodes** connected by weighted links. When training data flows through the network, it produces a prediction. The network then measures how wrong that prediction was — this is the **loss**.

To improve, the network runs **backpropagation**: it traces the error backwards through each layer, computing exactly how much each weight contributed to the mistake. An **optimizer** then nudges each weight in the direction that reduces the loss. Repeat this thousands of times and the network gradually gets better.

> **Key insight:** The network never explicitly learns rules. It discovers patterns purely by adjusting numbers.
key_takeaways: Neural networks learn by adjusting weights to minimise a loss function.
Backpropagation efficiently computes gradients using the chain rule.
An optimizer (e.g. gradient descent) applies those gradients to update weights.

diagrams.content:
---
title: Training Loop
---
flowchart LR
    A([Input Data]) --> B[Forward Pass]
    B --> C[Compute Loss]
    C --> D[Backpropagation]
    D --> E[Update Weights]
    E --> B
diagrams.caption: The neural network training loop

inline_questions.question_text: What is the main purpose of backpropagation?
options: To feed data forward through the network
To compute gradients so weights can be updated
To initialise the network's weights randomly
To evaluate the model on test data
inline_questions.explanation: Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight, enabling the optimizer to update the weights in the right direction.

inline_questions.question_text: Which of the following are components of a neural network training loop? (Select all that apply)
inline_questions.explanation: A training loop consists of: forward pass (prediction), loss computation (measure error), backpropagation (compute gradients), and weight update (apply gradients). Feature engineering is a pre-processing step, not part of the loop.

topic.title: Preventing Overfitting
topic.summary: Overfitting causes poor generalisation; regularisation techniques constrain model complexity to fix it.

topics.content_md:
## Preventing Overfitting

A model that scores 99% on training data but 60% on new data has **overfit** — it memorised the training examples instead of learning general patterns.

### Why it happens
When a network has too many parameters relative to training examples, it can learn the noise in the data as if it were a real signal.

### How to prevent it

| Technique | How it works |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Dropout** | Randomly disables neurons during training, forcing the network to learn redundant representations |
| **L2 Regularisation** | Adds a penalty for large weights, keeping them small and the model simpler |
| **Early Stopping** | Stops training when validation loss stops improving |

**Activation functions** also play a role: ReLU is preferred over Sigmoid in deep networks because it avoids the **vanishing gradient problem**, where gradients shrink to near-zero as they propagate back through many layers.
key_takeaways: Overfitting means the model memorises training data and fails to generalise.
Dropout, L2 regularisation, and early stopping are the main defences.
ReLU avoids the vanishing gradient problem that affects Sigmoid and Tanh.

diagrams.content:
---
title: Overfitting vs Generalisation
---
flowchart TD
    A[High Training Accuracy] --> B{High Validation Accuracy?}
    B -- Yes --> C([Good Generalisation])
    B -- No --> D([Overfitting])
    D --> E[Apply Regularisation]
    E --> A
diagrams.caption: Detecting and addressing overfitting

inline_questions.question_text: What does dropout do during training?
options: Removes low-importance features from the input
Randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons each step
Reduces the learning rate when loss plateaus
Stops training when validation loss increases
inline_questions.explanation: Dropout randomly sets a fraction of neuron outputs to zero during each training step. This prevents neurons from co-adapting too closely and forces the network to learn more robust, distributed representations.
